mnemonics.title: Pointer Arithmetic Operations
mnemonics.content: P.A.D.M. - POINTERS CAN: Add integer to pointer, DIFFERENCE two pointers (same array), Multiply/Divide NOT allowed

mnemonics.title: Array-Pointer Relationship
mnemonics.content: A.R.R.A.Y. - Array Replaces Right Address, Yes! (arr = &arr[0], arr+i = &arr[i], *arr = arr[0])

mnemonics.title: Increment/Decrement Pointer
mnemonics.content: P.O.S.T. - Pre-Increment changes Address, Post-Increment returns Old then moves

acronyms.expansion: Pointer Integer Promotion - Adding integer to pointer scales by sizeof(type)
acronyms.usage: p + n means p + (n * sizeof(*p)) bytes

acronyms.expansion: Scale Index Zero Offset Element Finder - determines step size in pointer arithmetic
Dereference Everything Returns Element From End - * operator accesses value at address
acronyms.usage: *p gives value, **p gives value at address stored in p

visualTips.visualization: Think of a pointer as an arrow pointing to a box. Adding 1 moves the arrow to the next box of same size. For int (4 bytes), the arrow moves 4 positions. For char (1 byte), it moves 1 position.
Draw consecutive memory blocks: |10|20|30|40|50|. A pointer to first block plus 2 (p+2) lands on the third block containing 30.
Think of array name as a fixed starting point on a street. arr+3 is the 3rd house from start. *(arr+3) is who lives in that house.

analogies.analogy: Like stepping stones in a river. Each step (adding 1) moves you forward by the size of one stone, not one inch. If stones are 2 feet apart, 3 steps take you 6 feet.
Like hotel room numbers. Room 101 is first floor first room. Adding 5 gives room 106 (not 105), because rooms are numbered sequentially accounting for floor size.
Like measuring distance between two mileposts. If milepost 50 and milepost 55 are on the same highway, distance is 5 miles - not the physical distance in feet.
